1: 'The Spirit expressly says that in later times some will depart from the faith, devoting themselves to deceitful spirits and teachings of demons' — the departure 'from the faith' is departure from faith in Christ; the deceitful spirits' teachings are Christologically distorting. The Spirit who speaks this warning is the Spirit of Christ (1 Peter 1:11), and the warning serves the protection of the Christ-confession. The 'later times' that the Spirit announces are the present age inaugurated by Christ's first coming and extending to his parousia.
2: 'Through the insincerity of liars whose consciences are seared' — the cauterized conscience has lost responsiveness to the Christ who stands at the door and knocks (Revelation 3:20). Christ as the truth (John 14:6) generates conscience in those who are being conformed to him; to sear the conscience is to suppress the Christ-truth that would otherwise produce moral discomfort. The false teachers' insincerity is Christological failure at the level of personal integrity.
3: 'Who forbid marriage and require abstinence from foods that God created to be received with thanksgiving by those who believe and know the truth' — the false teaching that rejects God's creation is a rejection of the Creator's goodness attested by Christ's own enjoyment of creation: he ate with sinners, turned water into wine, fed thousands with bread and fish. Christ's incarnation is the definitive theological statement that matter is good; rejecting food and marriage contradicts the Incarnation's witness to the goodness of creaturely existence.
4: 'For everything created by God is good, and nothing is to be rejected if it is received with thanksgiving' — the creation-goodness principle is the theological ground of the Incarnation: the God who declares all created things good is the God who takes on created flesh in Christ. Christ's embodied ministry — eating, drinking, sleeping, weeping, laughing — is the fullest possible endorsement of creation's goodness. The false teachers' rejection of creation implicitly rejects the Incarnation's vindication of the material order.
5: 'It is sanctified by the word of God and prayer' — the word of God that sanctifies food is the word that, in Christ, became flesh (John 1:14). The sanctification of ordinary food by thanksgiving-prayer is a Christological act: the believer who gives thanks does so through Christ (Colossians 3:17: whatever you do in word or deed, do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him). Every meal is Christologically consecrated in this sense.
6: 'If you put these things before the brothers, you will be a good servant (diakonos) of Christ Jesus, nourished on the words of the faith and of the good doctrine you have followed' — Timothy's servanthood is explicitly 'of Christ Jesus': he is Christ's minister, Christ's representative. The nourishment on 'words of the faith' is the Christological feeding that sustains him: immersion in the Christ-centered proclamation is the minister's own spiritual sustenance, just as Christ is the bread of life (John 6:35).
7: 'Have nothing to do with irreverent, silly myths; train yourself for godliness' — godliness (eusebeia) in the Pastoral Epistles is the Christological orientation of the whole person toward God; it is defined by the 'mystery of godliness' hymn (1 Timothy 3:16) where Christ himself is the content and embodiment of godliness. To train for godliness is to be formed by the pattern of the one in whom godliness is incarnated.
8: 'Bodily training is of some value, but godliness is valuable in every way, as it holds promise for the present life and also for the life to come' — the eschatological benefit of godliness reaches beyond death because it is participation in the life of the one who conquered death (John 11:25-26: I am the resurrection and the life; whoever lives and believes in me shall never die). The present-and-future promise of godliness reflects the already-and-not-yet structure of Christ's kingdom: the resurrection life is already present in the Spirit, not-yet fully consummated at his parousia.
9: 'The saying is trustworthy and deserving of full acceptance' — the faithful saying formula introduces or endorses a formulation of the Christological gospel that the community holds and transmits. The 'trustworthy' (pistos) content is trustworthy because Christ himself is faithful (pistos, 2 Timothy 2:13; Revelation 1:5; 1 Timothy 19:11); the formula's reliability is anchored in the reliability of the person the content proclaims.
10: 'We labor and strive because we have set our hope on the living God, who is the Savior of all people, especially of those who believe' — the 'living God' who is Savior is the God who raised Christ from the dead (Romans 4:24; 1 Peter 1:21). The hope that sustains apostolic labor is resurrection hope; the Savior in whom that hope is placed is the God who has already demonstrated his saving power in the resurrection of Christ. The 'especially of those who believe' distinguishes the scope of God's salvific intent (all people) from the realization of salvation (those who believe).
11: 'Command and teach these things' — the dual authority of apostolic command and reasoned teaching reflects Christ's own teaching authority: he taught as one who had authority (Matthew 7:29). Timothy's mandate to command and teach is derivative from Christ's authority; he speaks in Christ's name (1 Corinthians 5:4; Colossians 3:17: whatever you do in word or deed, do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus). The content commanded and taught is Christological: the faithful sayings, the mystery of godliness, the sound doctrine that derives from Christ.
12: 'Let no one despise you for your youth, but set the believers an example in speech, in conduct, in love, in faith, in purity' — the six-fold example Timothy is to model is the Christological character he is to embody and reproduce. Speech, conduct, love, faith, and purity describe Christ himself: his word was truth (John 14:6), his conduct was sinless (Hebrews 4:15), his love was self-giving (John 15:13), his faith was unwavering, his purity absolute. Timothy's example functions as a local embodiment of the Christ-pattern in the Ephesian community.
13: 'Devote yourself to the public reading of Scripture, to exhortation, to teaching' — the reading of Scripture in the assembly is reading the texts that witness to Christ (John 5:39: these are they that testify about me; Luke 24:27: beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, he interpreted to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself). The exhortation and teaching that follow are Christological interpretation: making the Christ-witness of the Scriptures available to the community.
14: 'Do not neglect the gift that is in you, which was given you by prophecy when the council of elders laid their hands on you' — the charismatic gift Timothy received is a Christological endowment: spiritual gifts are the Spirit of Christ at work in the community (1 Corinthians 12:4-6: there are varieties of gifts but the same Spirit, same Lord, same God). The ordination rite is a Christological commissioning: the one who received the gift serves Christ through it.
15: 'Practice these things; immerse yourself in them, so that everyone may see your progress' — the progress (prokopē) that others observe is progress in Christological formation: conformity to the image of Christ that the Spirit produces (2 Corinthians 3:18: we are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another). Timothy's visible progress is the community's evidence that the gospel of Christ produces the character it promises.
16: 'Keep a close watch on yourself and on the teaching. Persist in this, for by so doing you will save both yourself and your hearers' — the salvation of Timothy and his hearers is mediated through the faithful ministry of the word. The word proclaimed is the word of Christ (Colossians 3:16); the salvation it produces is Christ-salvation. Timothy's pastoral perseverance is in service of a Christ-centered salvific process: his continuing faithfulness extends the reach of Christ's saving power to those who hear.
